coredata.dc:title: Unveiling the multifaceted applications of pamoic acid carbon dots (PACDs) in sensing and oncology
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Abbasi, Ambreen
Bhat, Zia Ul Haq
Khan, Shamiuddin
Owais, Mohammad
Shakir, Mohammad
coredata.dc:description: In our research we explore the world of PACDs, carbon dots synthesized from pamoic acid through a single step pyrolysis method. Our findings reveal that PACDs have capabilities of serving as sensitive and selective sensors in both colorimetric and fluorescent modes. They are particularly effective, at colorimetrically and fluorometrically detecting ferric ions and can also act as fluorometric sensors for pH. When ferric ions are introduced an interesting transformation occurs. A noticeable change in color unfolds before our eyes, under 365 nm UV light the fluorescence shifts from green to blue while in daylight it changes from a yellow to a deep ink blue. Notably these detection techniques can precisely measure ferric ions within concentrations ranging from 5 µM to 80 µM with a detection limit of 0.1 µM for fluorescence response. Additionally, they can detect ferric ions colorimetrically within the range of 5 µM to 45 µM with a detection limit of 3.8 µM. Furthermore, the PACDs exhibit a capability to adapt to different pH levels. In alkaline environments with a pH range between 8 and 11 the fluorescence signal demonstrates a response that directly correlates with pH levels and slightly shifts its position. In contrast under acidic conditions a noticeable shift, towards blue is observed in the fluorescence signal leading to a change in color from green to blue when exposed to UV light. This shift persists as the fluorescence signal directly correlates with decreasing pH levels in settings. Apart from their proficiency in ferric ion detection and pH monitoring, the PACDs also demonstrate potential in cancer research. Through an assessment using the MTT assay it was discovered that the PACDs exhibit cytotoxic effects against five different cancer cell lines; HCT 116, MDA MB 231, Hep3B, MCF 7 and HeLa. The findings are promising as the PACDs show IC50 values of 12.5 µg/ml 6.25 µg/ml 25 µg/ml 50 µg/ml and 100 µg/ml for these cell lines. This research highlights the versatility and potential of PACDs as a tool, in both sensing applications and oncology research.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Green emitting carbon dots
Dual sensor
Ferric ions
pH sensor
The cytotoxic agent

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence response of PACDs towards different pH
Fluorescence and the colorimetric response of PACDs towards Fe3+ ions

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India
Department of Chemistry, University of York

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to extend their gratitude to the Department of Chemistry, at AMU for their support through programs like UGC DRS\u2013SAP, DST\u2013PURSE and DST\u2013FIST. A special appreciation goes to USIF AMU for assisting in TEM analysis. The authors also acknowledge SAIF MG University, Kottayam for conducting lifetime analysis, MG University for quantum yield measurements and MNIT Jaipur for their contributions, to DLS and zeta studies.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of PACDs (b) Size histogram of PACDs (c) SAED image of PACDs.
XPS spectra of PACDs (a) survey spectrum (b) High-resolution C1s spectrum (c) High-resolution O1s spectrum (d) High-resolution N1s spectrum.
(a) UV–Vis absorbance spectra of PACDs in different pH.(b) Fluorescence spectra of PACDs in different pH (c) Linear plot of acidic pH (d) Linear plot of basic pH.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of PACDs on addition of different Fe3+ ions concentrations (b) Stern Volmer plot (c) Absorbance spectra of PACDs on addition of different Fe3+ ions concentrations (d) Linear plot of absorbance.
Cell Viability of non-cancerous and cancer cells treated with different concentrations of the PACDs.
An overview on recently reported carbon dots-based methods for determination of Fe3+.

row.entry:
Colorimetric and
Fluorimetric
Colorimetric and
Fluorimetric

head.ce:title: Unveiling the multifaceted applications of pamoic acid carbon dots (PACDs) in sensing and oncology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Division of Inorganic Chemistry, Department of Chemistry, Aligarh Muslim University, Aligarh 202002, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Division of Inorganic Chemistry, Department of Chemistry, Aligarh Muslim University, Aligarh 202002, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Interdisciplinary Biotechnology Unit, Aligarh Muslim University, Aligarh 202002, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Interdisciplinary Biotechnology Unit, Aligarh Muslim University, Aligarh 202002, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In our research we explore the world of PACDs, carbon dots synthesized from pamoic acid through a single step pyrolysis method. Our findings reveal that PACDs have capabilities of serving as sensitive and selective sensors in both colorimetric and fluorescent modes. They are particularly effective, at colorimetrically and fluorometrically detecting ferric ions and can also act as fluorometric sensors for pH.

When ferric ions are introduced an interesting transformation occurs. A noticeable change in color unfolds before our eyes, under 365 nm UV light the fluorescence shifts from green to blue while in daylight it changes from a yellow to a deep ink blue. Notably these detection techniques can precisely measure ferric ions within concentrations ranging from 5 µM to 80 µM with a detection limit of 0.1 µM for fluorescence response. Additionally, they can detect ferric ions colorimetrically within the range of 5 µM to 45 µM with a detection limit of 3.8 µM.

Furthermore, the PACDs exhibit a capability to adapt to different pH levels. In alkaline environments with a pH range between 8 and 11 the fluorescence signal demonstrates a response that directly correlates with pH levels and slightly shifts its position. In contrast under acidic conditions a noticeable shift, towards blue is observed in the fluorescence signal leading to a change in color from green to blue when exposed to UV light. This shift persists as the fluorescence signal directly correlates with decreasing pH levels in settings.

Apart from their proficiency in ferric ion detection and pH monitoring, the PACDs also demonstrate potential in cancer research. Through an assessment using the MTT assay it was discovered that the PACDs exhibit cytotoxic effects against five different cancer cell lines; HCT 116, MDA MB 231, Hep3B, MCF 7 and HeLa. The findings are promising as the PACDs show IC50 values of 12.5 µg/ml 6.25 µg/ml 25 µg/ml 50 µg/ml and 100 µg/ml for these cell lines. This research highlights the versatility and potential of PACDs as a tool, in both sensing applications and oncology research.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer, a disease caused by cell growth continues to be a major cause of death, in even the most advanced countries despite significant advancements in medical science [1]. Traditional cancer treatments like radiation therapy and chemotherapy often have effects on the well-being of patients. For example, chemotherapy can harm cells along with cells leading to unpleasant side effects such as pale skin, nausea and hair loss [2]. Therefore, it is crucial to develop effective cancer drugs and therapies.

One promising approach in this pursuit is the use of carbon dots (CDs) which're nanostructures made up of carbon atoms arranged in a graphitic core. These structures are usually less than 20 nm in size [3]. What makes them particularly interesting is their ability to penetrate barriers effectively making them highly suitable, for biomedical applications [4]. Moreover, numerous studies have shown that carbon dots can trigger apoptosis (cell death) and significantly hinder the growth of cancer cells through pathways.

For example, the research conducted by Jufeng Xia and colleagues emphasizes the effectiveness of carbon dots derived from beans. They have demonstrated how these carbon dots can reduce cell viability and inhibit the migration of a 16 cancer cell lines [5]. On the other hand Elham and their team have utilized carbon dots synthesized from walnut oil to establish their cytotoxicity against two specific cancer cell lines, MCF-7 and PC-3 which was confirmed through the MTT method [6]. S. Simsek and collaborators have also explored cytotoxicity assessment using carbon dots extracted from Nerium Oleander leaves. They found that these carbon dots elicited in vitro responses, against MCF 7 and HDFa cancer cell lines [7]. These diverse approaches bring hope for advancements in our fight, against cancer.

In the network of living systems ferric ions are the common essential trace ions. They play a role, in physiological processes and the formation of important structures like myoglobin and haemoglobin [8,9]. However, the excessive presence of ions in water resources poses a threat to human health leading to conditions such as cancer and organ degeneration especially in the heart and liver [10,11]. Therefore, there is a need for cost effective and highly efficient methods to detect ferric ions.

Addressing this need for detection techniques Jingjing and their team have introduced an approach using carbon dots created by combining maleic acid and urea through microwave heating. These carbon dots act as indicators with a detection range of 0.02 mg/ml to 0.08 mg/ml and an incredibly low limit of detection at just 2.6 µM [12]. Similarly, Xin Zhang and their group have introduced nitrogen doped fluorescent carbon dots that exhibit sensitivity as indicators, for ferric ions and their detection range spans, from 1.0 µM to 80 µM and their detection limit is 1 µM [13]. Many studies have utilized carbon dots sourced from origins, both natural and synthetic, to unlock the mysteries of ferric ion detection through fluorescence and colorimetry14−23. These diverse approaches shed light on a path towards ferric ion detection that is efficient cost effective and vital for safeguarding human health. Going beyond single mode assays the scientific community has shown interest in dual mode detection techniques. Various strategies combining fluorescent/SERS/, colorimetric/SERS and fluorescent/magnetic methods have been explored. Of these approaches special attention has been given to the field of fluorescent/ colorimetric dual-mode sensing due, to its ability to not only visually detect specific analytes but also provide highly responsive fluorescent assays [14,15].
Building upon the aforementioned insights our present investigation focuses on synthesizing carbon dots derived from pamoic acid. Throughout our study we will refer to these as PACDs.
These PACDs possess a range of qualities that deserve attention;

(i) It is worth noting that the use of pamoic acid, in the synthesis of carbon dots is unexplored. (ii) The fabrication process follows an efficient one step pyrolysis method highlighting its ease. (iii) The potential of the PACDs as cancer agents has been carefully examined with successful results against five different cancer cell lines. Excitingly these carbon dots demonstrate toxicity against cancer cells while leaving PBMCs and RBC cells unharmed offering a potential breakthrough in cancer treatment. (iv) In addition to their oncological applications these carbon dots also show promise as sensors. They are capable of detecting ferric ions using both fluorescence and colorimetric methods showcasing their versatility. Notably, their fluorescence response changes from green to blue in acidic environments while demonstrating fluorescence enhancement across both acidic and alkaline pH levels. These characteristics highlight the functional nature of the synthesized carbon dots and suggest advancements, in cancer therapy and analytical sensing applications.
The synthetic procedure and other experimental methods are given in the supplementary materials of this manuscript, however, brief summary of synthesis process is given below

The PACDs were synthesised by simple one step pyrolysis method. A paste of 2gm pamoic acid was made with 20 % ethylenediamine solution and was kept in muffle furnace and heated up to 190 °C and was kept at that temperature for an hour. After an hour a dark brown residue was obtained which was repeatedly washed with water to remove excess of ethylenediamine and was dissolved in 30 ml ethanol and was subjected to centrifugation for 20 min. The supernatant obtained was than subjected to dialysis (MWCO = 1000D) for 6 h. The purified solution was kept in oven at 60 °C to obtain PACDs powder. The weight of obtained PACDs was 1.2 gm.

To gain insights, into the shape and size of the PACDs we conducted an analysis using Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM). In Fig. 1 a an intriguing TEM image revealed that the PACDs have a spherical shape and are clearly distinguishable from one another. Fig. 1b presents a constructed size histogram based on analysis of approximately 40 individual particles. It is evident that the PACDs exhibit a range of sizes ranging from a 5 nm to a larger 20 nm with an average size of about 15 nm, which is quite fascinating. When examining the PACDs through high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM), no distinct fringes were observed, indicating their amorphous nature. Additionally, Fig. 1c displays a selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern with diffuse rings further confirming the amorphous structure, within the PACDs.

The XRD graph, in Fig. 2 shows two peaks at 25° and 42° which are associated with the lattice planes (002) and (100) of carbon-based materials respectively [16]. The peak at an angle of 25° corresponds, to the (002) plane with an an interlayer spacing of around 0.36 nm. This increased spacing exceeding that found in bulk graphite (0.34 nm) is believed to be a result of the abundance of functional groups located at the edges of the PACDs [17].

The FT IR spectrum of PACDs can be seen in Fig. S1. It is evident that there are peaks, at 3220 cm−1 and 3052 cm−1, which correspond to the stretching vibrations of OH and NH bonds. Another distinctive feature is the peak at 1633 cm−1 indicating the stretching vibrations of CO bonds. Additionally, we can observe the presence of alkyl CH stretching at 2933 cm−1. Further examination reveals the bending vibration of OH bonds at 1360 cm−1 while there are peaks at 1515 cm−1 and 1470 cm−1 originating from the benzene skeleton. An interesting observation is that CN stretching vibrations are present in PACDs marked at 1389 cm−1 [18]. These findings collectively confirm a presence of functional groups containing oxygen and nitrogen on the surface of PACDs.

To determine the composition and nitrogen content within PACDs we conducted XPS analysis. As depicted in Fig. 3 PACDs consist primarily of three elements; carbon (71.01 %) oxygen (19.01 %) and nitrogen (9.98 %). The corresponding binding energies, for these elements were measured as follows; C1s (284 eV) N1s (390 eV) and O1s (530 eV). Moreover, in Fig. 3 we are presented with high resolution spectra, for C1s, N1s and O1s. These spectra provide insights into chemical states and bonding configurations. In the C1s spectrum we can observe three peaks at 284 eV, 285.7 eV and 287.7 eV, each of these peaks corresponds to functionalities; CC/CC, C N and CO. Moving on to the O1s spectrum it exhibits two distinctive peaks at 530.7 eV and 531 eV indicating the presence of CO and CO groups. The N1s spectrum reveals three peaks at 397.8 eV, 399 eV and 399.8 eV. These peaks signify the existence of pyridinic N, NH2 and pyrrolic N respectively [19]. The analysis through XPS confirms the incorporation of nitrogen, into PACDs while shedding light on their chemical structure and functionalities.

The negative zeta potential of 14.8 mV observed in the PACDs indicates the presence of hydroxyl, carboxyl and amino groups, on the carbon dots surface. This aligns with the expected chemical functionalities.

The thermal behavior and stability of the PACDs were thoroughly examined, as shown in Fig. S2. The thermogram revealed a multi-step degradation process. Initially a slight weight loss of 2.5 % between 75 °C and 100 °C can be attributed to moisture evaporation. Subsequent stages showed weight losses at temperature intervals; 256 °C to 430 °C (61 %) 430 °C to 553 °C (6.8 %) and 553 °C to 694 °C (27.4 %). These degradations indicate the cleavage of surface bound groups ultimately leading to the structural disintegration of the entire carbon dot framework. This detailed thermal analysis provides insights, into the stability and thermal behavior of the PACDs.

The UV Vis spectrum of PACDs beautifully shown in Fig. S3 reveals a pattern. It displays peaks with two shoulders that span from 325 to 375 nm. These shoulder peaks corresponds to the π-π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains and the n-π* transition of CO and CN groups [20].

Shifting our attention to the emission spectrum of PACDs (as depicted in Fig. S3) we discover a pattern. When excited at a wavelength of 360 nm this spectrum exhibits two peaks at 404 nm and 426 nm accompanied by a broader peak at 505 nm. The presence of these emission peaks, under an single excitation wavelength is uncommon. Additionally, these emission peaks remain relatively consistent when the excitation wavelength varies between 300 nm to 430 nm; however, it's important to note that their intensity does change as illustrated in Fig. S4.

Moreover, using a integrated sphere method we have determined that the absolute quantum yield of PACDs to be 14.11 %. Another intriguing aspect is the behavior exhibited by solutions containing PACDs. In daylight they display a shade of bright yellow but under UV light, with a wavelength of 360 nm (as shown in the insets of Fig. S3a and b) they emit a vibrant and captivating green glow adding an intriguing aspect, to their visual properties.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of PACDs towards different pH
ce:section.ce:para: In our study, we observed that the photophysical properties of carbon dots are profoundly influenced by the pH of the medium, inducing significant changes in both their optical characteristics and hydrodynamic size.

Under acidic conditions, as the pH was reduced from pH 7 to pH 6, the carbon dots exhibited a decrease in absorbance (Fig. 4 a), indicating a reduction in the light absorption capabilities. This decrease in absorbance was paralleled by a dramatic alteration in the fluorescence spectrum (Fig. 4b): the emission peak at 505 nm nearly vanished, giving way to a new peak at 446 nm that grew stronger with decreasing pH. This shift, indicative of the protonation of surface functional groups, led to a restructuring of the electronic landscape of the carbon dots, visually observed as a transition from a light yellow to an almost colorless appearance in daylight (Inset of Fig. 4a), and from green to blue under 365 nm UV light (Inset of Fig. 4b). The fluorescence signal's shape also underwent a noticeable transformation between pH 7 and 6, stabilizing at lower pH levels. This behavior indicates a stabilization of the carbon dots' fluorescent state post an initial pH-induced perturbation. The physical response, manifested as an increase in hydrodynamic size, suggests proton-induced swelling or aggregation phenomena.

Upon increasing the pH to alkaline conditions, we noted an opposite trend. The absorbance of the carbon dots increased (Fig. 4a), suggesting that deprotonation restores the electronic transitions responsible for light absorption. This increase in absorbance was accompanied by a fading of the emission peaks at 404 nm and 426 nm and an intensification and slight blue shift of the primary fluorescence peak from 505 nm to 499 nm (Fig. 4b). These changes suggest that the deprotonation at higher pH levels reactivates electronic states conducive to fluorescence, possibly by reversing the structural changes induced by protonation.

Throughout the pH range, the absorbance peaks at 341 nm and 363 nm (Fig. 4a) consistently displayed slight blue and red shifts, respectively, with a broadening effect, indicating the sensitivity of the carbon dots' electronic transitions to surface protonation and deprotonation dynamics.

Under both acidic and basic conditions we observed an increase, in the hydrodynamic size of the carbon dots from 26 nm in neutral medium to 75 nm in acidic medium and to 90 nm in basic medium. This growth can be attributed to the interaction between protons or hydroxyl ions and the surface groups which lead to expanded hydration shells due to their interaction or aggregation caused by changes in pH induced surface charges. Interestingly our study found a linear relationship between fluorescence intensity and pH levels. This relationship is depicted in Fig. 4c and Fig. 4d of our study. It indicates that changes in pH systematically affect the processes governing fluorescence intensity showcasing the potential of carbon dots, as fluorescent probes to pH variations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence and the colorimetric response of PACDs towards Fe3+ ions
ce:section.ce:para: The research focused on investigating how 12 different metal ions affect the brightness of PACDs as shown in Fig. S5. Among these metal ions Fe3+ had the impact, on reducing the fluorescence of PACDs while the other metal ions had a minimal effect. Interestingly at concentrations of up to 400 µM no noticeable interference was observed from other metal ions (Fig. S5). This unique selectivity of PACDs for Fe3+ ions can be attributed to how quickly Fe3 + ions bind with the nitrogen and oxygen containing groups within PACDs.

As a result PACDs show specificity for detecting Fe3+ ions compared to metal ions. Therefore, they hold promise as a sensor for “turning off” fluorescence caused by Fe3+ ions. The study also investigated how well PACDs can sense concentrations of Fe3+ ions ranging from 0 µM to 103 µM as shown in Fig. 5 a. As the concentration of Fe3+ ions increases there is a decrease in fluorescence intensity, at wavelengths of 404 nm, 426 nm and 505 nm.

Photographs were taken of a solution containing PACDs under 360 nm excitation (as shown in the image, in Fig. 5a). These photos vividly demonstrate how the fluorescence color of the PACDs solution changes from green to blue as the concentration of Fe3+ ions increases. The decrease in fluorescence due to the presence of Fe3+ follows the known Stern Volmer equation; F0/F = 1 + Ksv[Fe3+]. Here F0 represents the fluorescence intensity without Fe3+ ions, F represents the fluorescence intensity with Fe3+ and Ksv is the Stern Volmer constant. When examining the Stern Volmer plot we observed a linear relationship within a concentration range of 5 µM to 80 µM (Fig. 5b). The regression coefficient (R2) was high at 0.9931. The corresponding Ksv value was found to be 1.3259 × 104 M−1. However. deviations from linearity were noticed at higher concentrations of Fe3+ (inset in Fig. 5b). The calculated bimolecular quenching rate kq impressively stands at a value of 1.59 × 1012 L mol−1 s−1 significantly exceeding the upper limit, for collision quenching constants set at 2.0 × 1010 L mol−1 s−1.

Additionally, when Fe3+ is introduced the average fluorescence lifetime of PACDs shows a change from 8.32 ns to 8.55 ns at 505 nm and, from 8.46 ns to 8.55 ns at 426 nm (as shown in Fig. S6). Based on the Stern − Volmer constant, the bimolecular quenching rate constant and the consistent maintenance of fluorescence lifetime it can be reasonably inferred that the quenching of fluorescence caused by Fe3+ ions primarily occur due to quenching resulting from the formation of a stable complex between PACDs surface functional groups and Fe3+ ions. This interpretation is further supported by UV– Vis absorption experiments conducted on PACDs with varying concentrations of Fe3+ ions (illustrated in Fig. 5c) revealing absorption changes as Fe3+ ions concentration increase. These findings indicate that fluorescence quenching is due to quenching resulting from the establishment of a complex between PACDs and Fe3+. Moreover, an impressive detection limit of 0.1 µM was achieved with a signal, to noise ratio of 3 highlighting the sensors sensitivity and wide linear range.

The colorimetric properties of PACDs were investigated by observing changes, in color when different metal ions were introduced. An interesting observation was made when Fe3+ was added to the PACD solution resulting in a color transformation (shown in the inset of Fig. 5c). Initially the solution changed from yellow to a brown shade at lower concentrations of Fe3+ ions and eventually turned into a deep ink blue hue at higher concentrations (as depicted in the inset of Fig. 5c). This remarkable change in color was quite distinct compared to the effect observed when other metals were introduced into the solution (Fig. S7).
The detection limit meticulously calculated for this response was 3.8 µM (as demonstrated in Fig. 5d).

The transformation in both colorimetry and fluorimetry observed in the PACDs solution upon adding Fe3+ ions may be attributed to several factors at play. These include intricate interactions, charge transfer processes between PACDs and Fe3+, modifications in surface states and aggregation of carbon dots. To provide evidence, for these explanations we conducted investigations including UV/vis absorption spectra analysis, zeta potential measurements and dynamic light scattering (DLS) studies.

When we gradually introduced Fe3+ ions into our carbon dot system we noticed changes, in the absorption spectrum (as shown in Fig. 5c) in the 325–375 nm range, which is characteristic of these carbon dots (referred to as PACDs). One noticeable change was an increase in the intensity of the peaked absorption bands within this range. The peak at 341 nm remained prominent indicating its nature or a specific interaction with Fe3+ ions that preserves or enhances this feature. This particular peak likely corresponds to transitions within the carbon dots, such as π π* transitions of CC bonds, which may not be significantly affected by the presence of Fe3+ ions.

In contrast we observed that the peak at 363 nm became less noticeable when Fe3+ ions were added. This suggests an interaction or interference between Fe3+ ions and the electronic states or surface groups of the carbon dots contributing to this absorption feature. The decrease in intensity at 363 nm could be attributed to factors, including the formation of complexes between Fe3+ ions and functional groups on the carbon dots or partial suppression of transitions associated with this absorption peak.

The observation of an absorption band, along with its shift from 589 nm to 601 nm as the concentration of ferric ions increases is crucial. This band indicates the formation of a charge transfer complex between the carbon dots and ferric ions. In this process electronic density partially moves from the donor (carbon dots) to the acceptor (Fe3+ ions) resulting in absorption characteristics. As the concentration of Fe3+ increases there is a shift towards wavelengths indicating a stronger interaction or a higher degree of charge transfer between ferric ions and carbon dots. This is supported by the change in color of carbon dots from yellow to brown at lower concentrations of ferric ions ultimately transitioning to an ink blue shade at higher concentrations. These alterations in the UV Vis absorption spectrum suggest changes in both surface and internal electronic configurations of carbon dots due to their formation of complex with ions and subsequent shifts, in electronic transitions.

After introducing Fe3+ ions a noticeable change, in the zeta value was observed, shifting from 14.5 mV to + 16.5 mV. This indicates that Fe3+ coordinates with the carboxyl, hydroxyl and amino groups on the surface of PACDs. These formed complexes may have distinct electronic properties compared to their individual components leading to changes in the color characteristics of the PACDs.

Additionally, the interaction between Fe3+ ions and PACDs can cause aggregation or clustering of the PACDs. This is supported by an increase in the size of PACDs from 26 nm to 84 nm. The state of aggregation strongly influences the properties of PACDs. Aggregated PACDs may exhibit different fluorescence shades compared to individual PACDs due to changes in electronic states and energy transfer processes occurring within these aggregates.

In our observations we found that the fluorescence peak broadens (Fig. 5a) indicating an increased range of emitting states activated in the presence of ions. This phenomenon could be attributed to Fe3+ interacting with groups or surface states on carbon dots resulting in a wider distribution of electronic states and thus a more diverse emission spectrum.

Moreover, the widening of the peak may indicate a level of diversity within the system which could be a result of either carbon dots aggregating or the formation of various complexes involving carbon dots and Fe3+ ions. Furthermore, the observed shift in fluorescence color denotes a change in the dominant emission wavelength. Moving from green (approximately 505 nm) to blue (around 457 nm) suggests a shift towards shorter wavelengths, indicative of a blue shift in the fluorescence spectrum. This shift may be attributed to the interaction between Fe3+ ions and carbon dots potentially affecting the structure of carbon dots and leading to emissions with higher energy states.

It's worth highlighting that the significant sensitivity of PACDs towards Fe3+ ions was particularly pronounced at pH 7. At this pH level we observed fluorescence quenching and noticeable changes in color that were visible both to the naked eye and under UV light. Several factors can explain this behavior at pH 7. Firstly, at lower pH levels protonation of coordinating groups could limit coordination sites availability thus reducing interactions, between PACDs and Fe3+ ions. On the hand when the pH levels are higher it is possible for the hydroxides of ions to disrupt the coordination process. This can reduce both the quenching and color changing effects that are typically seen at a pH of 7. Comparison of our reported method with other reported work for detection of Fe3 + ions is given in Table 1 .

The cytotoxic potential of PACDs was evaluated using five cancer cell lines; HCT-116, MDA MB-231, HeLa, MCF-7 and Hep3B. Normal peripheral blood mononuclear cells (PBMCs) were also included as a reference, for cell behavior. To assess viability the MTT assay was used in this analysis. The results consistently showed a decrease in viability across all cancer cell lines as the concentration of PACDs in, µg/ml increased. This trend can be observed visually in Fig. 6 .

In contrast PBMCs did not exhibit any cytotoxicity even at the higher concentration of 400 µg/ml. The cell viability remained approximately at 90 % (Fig. 6). This finding emphasizes the promise of PACDs, in cancer therapy as they selectively impact cancer cells while sparing normal ones from damage.

To further confirm that PACDs do not harm normal cells we conducted an assessment using the Red Blood Cells (RBC) haemolysis assay (Fig. S8). This method involved exposing RBCs to increasing concentrations of PACDs, which revealed a hemolysis rate of around 16 % at the concentration of 256 µg/ml. This reinforces the idea that PACDs are non toxic to cellular components.

Moreover, determining IC50 values provided insights into how PACDs affect cancer cell lines. The values were found to be 12.5 µg/ml, 6.25 µg/ml, 25 µg/ml, 50 µg/ml and 100 µg/ml for HCT- 116 MDA- MB- 231, Hep3B, MCF-7 and HeLa cell lines. These results highlight the high cytotoxicity of PACDs against HCT-116 and MDA-MB-231 cell lines, while also demonstrating significant performance, against other cell lines as well.

Based on the response observed in the MDA-MB-231 cell lines we conducted a detailed analysis of morphological changes before and, after administering 80 µg/ml PACDs. As shown in Fig. S9, the results revealed an occurrence where cell apoptosis entirely occurred following PACDs treatment. These significant findings have opened up an avenue for investigation prompting us to delve deeper into understanding the underlying cellular mechanisms. Our future research endeavors will focus on unraveling the dynamics and interactions associated with PACDs cytotoxic effects.

To summarize, this study employed pamoic acid as the carbon source and ethylenediamine as the nitrogen source to synthesize a type of carbon dots known as PACDs. Through characterization using analytical techniques we gained insights into their properties and structure. Notably, these synthesized PACDs exhibited functionality as dual sensors for Fe3+ ions. They displayed changes in color under both UV light and normal daylight upon introducing Fe3+ ions, which can be attributed to their interaction with them. Furthermore, the PACDs demonstrated pH sensing capabilities by exhibiting different responses, across both acidic and basic pH ranges. This indicates that they could be useful, as pH sensors in a range of applications.

Additionally, our research uncovered that the PACDs demonstrated toxicity towards five cancer cell lines. These findings imply that PACDs may have applications in cancer treatment or other biomedical fields. In conclusion this study introduces PACDs as a nanomaterial with the ability to sense both Fe3+ ions and pH changes and also highlights their promising toxicity, against cancer cells.

Ambreen Abbasi: Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Zia Ul Haq Bhat: Writing – review & editing. Shamiuddin Khan: Investigation. Mohammad Owais: Resources, Investigation. Mohammad Shakir: Resources, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to extend their gratitude to the Department of Chemistry, at AMU for their support through programs like UGC DRS–SAP, DST–PURSE and DST–FIST. A special appreciation goes to USIF AMU for assisting in TEM analysis. The authors also acknowledge SAIF MG University, Kottayam for conducting lifetime analysis, MG University for quantum yield measurements and MNIT Jaipur for their contributions, to DLS and zeta studies.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2024.124564.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots- a study of its cytotoxic activity against HepG2 and MCF-7 cell lines
Mater. Today Proc.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Muktha, R. Sharath, N. Kottam, H.M. Kumarswamy, S. Srinath, Carbon dots- A study of its cytotoxic activity against HepG2 and MCF-7 cell lines, Mater. Today Proc. 49 (2021) 608–612. doi: 10.1016/j.matpr.2021.04.515.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A strategic review on carbon quantum dots for cancer-diagnostics and treatment, front. bioeng
Biotechnol.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Naik, S. Chaudhary, L. Ye, A.S. Parmar, A Strategic Review on Carbon Quantum Dots for Cancer-Diagnostics and Treatment, Front. Bioeng. Biotechnol. 10 (2022) 1–21. doi: 10.3389/fbioe.2022.882100.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots as promising tools for cancer diagnosis and therapy
Cancers (basel).

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G. Nocito, G. Calabrese, S. Forte, S. Petralia, C. Puglisi, M. Campolo, E. Esposito, S. Conoci, Carbon Dots as Promising Tools for Cancer Diagnosis and Therapy., Cancers (Basel). 13 (2021). doi: 10.3390/cancers13091991.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fasting and cancer treatment in humans: a case series report.
Aging (Albany NY)

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F.M. Safdie, T. Dorff, D. Quinn, L. Fontana, M. Wei, C. Lee, P. Cohen, V.D. Longo, Fasting and cancer treatment in humans: A case series report., Aging (Albany. NY). 1 (2009) 988–1007. doi: 10.18632/aging.100114.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots have antitumor action as monotherapy or combination therapy
Drug Discov. Ther.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Xia, Y. Kawamura, T. Suehiro, Y. Chen, K. Sato, Carbon dots have antitumor action as monotherapy or combination therapy., Drug Discov. Ther. 13 (2019) 114–117. doi: 10.5582/ddt.2019.01013.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Green synthesis of carbon dots derived from walnut oil and an investigation of their cytotoxic and apoptogenic activities toward cancer cells
Adv. Pharm. Bull.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: E. Arkan, A. Barati, M. Rahmanpanah, L. Hosseinzadeh, S. Moradi, M. Hajialyani, Green synthesis of carbon dots derived from walnut oil and an investigation of their cytotoxic and apoptogenic activities toward cancer cells, Adv. Pharm. Bull. 8 (2018) 149–155. doi: 10.15171/apb.2018.018.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: DNA-damage and cell cycle arrest initiated anti-cancer potency of super tiny carbon dots on MCF7 cell line
Sci. Rep.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Şimşek, A.A. Şüküroğlu, D. Yetkin, B. Özbek, D. Battal, R. Genç, DNA-damage and cell cycle arrest initiated anti-cancer potency of super tiny carbon dots on MCF7 cell line, Sci. Rep. 10 (2020) 1–14. doi: 10.1038/s41598-020-70796-3.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fe3+-sensitive carbon dots for detection of Fe3+ in aqueous solution and intracellular imaging of Fe3+ inside fungal cells
Front. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Chen, X. Sun, W. Pan, G. Yu, J. Wang, Fe3+-Sensitive Carbon Dots for Detection of Fe3+ in Aqueous Solution and Intracellular Imaging of Fe3+ Inside Fungal Cells , Front. Chem. . 7 (2020). https://www.frontiersin.org/articles/10.3389/fchem.2019.00911.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fabrication of multi-functional carbon dots based on “one stone, three birds” strategy and their applications for the dual-mode Fe3+detection, effective promotion on cell proliferation and treatment on ferric toxicosis: in vitro
J. Mater. Chem. B.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Cai, H. Xu, H. Chu, J. Li, D. Zhang, Fabrication of multi-functional carbon dots based on “one stone, three birds” strategy and their applications for the dual-mode Fe3+detection, effective promotion on cell proliferation and treatment on ferric toxicosis: In vitro, J. Mater. Chem. B. 9 (2021) 767–782. doi: 10.1039/d0tb02325f.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Detection of Fe3+ ions in aqueous environment using fluorescent carbon quantum dots synthesized from endosperm of Borassus flabellifer
Environ. Res.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Nagaraj, S. Ramalingam, C. Murugan, S. Aldawood, J.O. Jin, I. Choi, M. Kim, Detection of Fe3+ ions in aqueous environment using fluorescent carbon quantum dots synthesized from endosperm of Borassus flabellifer, Environ. Res. 212 (2022) 113273. doi: 10.1016/j.envres.2022.113273.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile synthesis of fluorescent carbon quantum dots from Betel leafs (Piper betle) for Fe3+sensing
Mater. Today Proc.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Raja, D. Sundaramurthy, Facile synthesis of fluorescent carbon quantum dots from Betel leafs (Piper betle) for Fe3+sensing, Mater. Today Proc. 34 (2021) 488–492. doi: doi: 10.1016/j.matpr.2020.03.096.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: N-doped carbon dots as fluorescent “Turn-Off” nanosensors for ascorbic acid and Fe3+ detection
ACS Appl. Nano Mater.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Cui, X. Zhu, Y. Liu, L. Liang, Y. Peng, S. Wu, Y. Zhao, N-Doped Carbon Dots as Fluorescent “Turn-Off” Nanosensors for Ascorbic Acid and Fe3+ Detection, ACS Appl. Nano Mater. 5 (2022) 7268–7277. doi: 10.1021/acsanm.2c01170.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A nitrogen-doped carbon dots based fluorescent nanosensor for sensitive assay of Fe3+ ions in Dioscorea opposita Thunb
Ind. Crops Prod.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Zhang, L. Fu, Y. Hou, N. Long, G. Zhu, X. Liao, L. Zhou, J. Lu, W. Kong, A nitrogen-doped carbon dots based fluorescent nanosensor for sensitive assay of Fe3+ ions in Dioscorea opposita Thunb, Ind. Crops Prod. 177 (2022) 114439. doi: doi: 10.1016/j.indcrop.2021.114439.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Red Emission B, N, S-co-doped carbon dots for colorimetric and fluorescent dual mode detection of Fe3+ ions in complex biological fluids and living cells
ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Liu, W. Duan, W. Song, J. Liu, C. Ren, J. Wu, D. Liu, H. Chen, Red Emission B, N, S-co-Doped Carbon Dots for Colorimetric and Fluorescent Dual Mode Detection of Fe3+ Ions in Complex Biological Fluids and Living Cells, ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces. 9 (2017) 12663–12672. doi: 10.1021/acsami.6b15746.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Dual-mode colorimetric and fluorometric probe for ferric ion detection using N-doped carbon dots prepared via hydrothermal synthesis followed by microwave irradiation
Opt. Mater. (amst)

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K.M. Omer, M. Sartin, Dual-mode colorimetric and fluorometric probe for ferric ion detection using N-doped carbon dots prepared via hydrothermal synthesis followed by microwave irradiation, Opt. Mater. (Amst). 94 (2019) 330–336. doi: 10.1016/j.optmat.2019.05.045.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Preparation of activated carbon decorated with carbon dots and its electrochemical performance
J. Ind. Eng. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.-J. Jang, Y.C. Kang, K.C. Roh, Preparation of activated carbon decorated with carbon dots and its electrochemical performance, J. Ind. Eng. Chem. 82 (2020) 383–389. doi: doi: 10.1016/j.jiec.2019.11.003.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Waste frying oil as a precursor for one-step synthesis of sulfur-doped carbon dots with pH-sensitive photoluminescence
Carbon N. Y.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Hu, J. Yang, J. Tian, L. Jia, J.-S. Yu, Waste frying oil as a precursor for one-step synthesis of sulfur-doped carbon dots with pH-sensitive photoluminescence, Carbon N. Y. 77 (2014) 775–782. doi: doi: 10.1016/j.carbon.2014.05.081.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ratiometric fluorescent detection of chromium(VI) in real samples based on dual emissive carbon dots
Talanta.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Ma, Y. Chen, J. Liu, Y. Han, S. Ma, X. Chen, Ratiometric fluorescent detection of chromium(VI) in real samples based on dual emissive carbon dots, Talanta. 185 (2018) 249–257. doi: doi: 10.1016/j.talanta.2018.03.081.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Spectroscopic fingerprints of graphitic, pyrrolic, pyridinic, and chemisorbed nitrogen in N-doped graphene
J. Phys. Chem. C.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Lazar, R. Mach, M. Otyepka, Spectroscopic Fingerprints of Graphitic, Pyrrolic, Pyridinic, and Chemisorbed Nitrogen in N-Doped Graphene, J. Phys. Chem. C. 123 (2019) 10695–10702. doi: 10.1021/acs.jpcc.9b02163.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Optical properties of pH-sensitive carbon-dots with different modifications
J. Lumin.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. Kong, H. Wu, Z. Ye, R. Li, T. Xu, B. Zhang, Optical properties of pH-sensitive carbon-dots with different modifications, J. Lumin. 148 (2014) 238–242. doi: doi: 10.1016/j.jlumin.2013.12.007.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hybrid carbon source for producing nitrogen-doped polymer nanodots: one-pot hydrothermal synthesis, fluorescence enhancement and highly selective detection of Fe(iii)
Nanoscale.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T. Lai, E. Zheng, L. Chen, X. Wang, L. Kong, C. You, Y. Ruan, X. Weng, Hybrid carbon source for producing nitrogen-doped polymer nanodots: one-pot hydrothermal synthesis, fluorescence enhancement and highly selective detection of Fe(iii), Nanoscale. 5 (2013) 8015–8021. doi: 10.1039/C3NR02014B.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor based on water soluble carbon nanodots with multiple sensing capacities
Nanoscale.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Qu, H. Chen, X. Zheng, J. Cao, X. Liu, Ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor based on water soluble carbon nanodots with multiple sensing capacities, Nanoscale. 5 (2013) 5514–5518. doi: 10.1039/C3NR00619K.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: H. Ding, J.-S. Wei, H.-M. Xiong, Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots with strong blue luminescence †, 6 (2014) 13817. doi: 10.1039/c4nr04267k.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facilely synthesized N-doped carbon quantum dots with high fluorescent yield for sensing Fe3+
New J. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Yu, C. Xu, Z. Tian, Y. Lin, Z. Shi, Facilely synthesized N-doped carbon quantum dots with high fluorescent yield for sensing Fe3+, New J. Chem. 40 (2016) 2083–2088. doi: 10.1039/C5NJ03252K.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Preparation of highly photoluminescent sulfur-doped carbon dots for Fe(iii) detection
J. Mater. Chem. A.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Q. Xu, P. Pu, J. Zhao, C. Dong, C. Gao, Y. Chen, J. Chen, Y. Liu, H. Zhou, Preparation of highly photoluminescent sulfur-doped carbon dots for Fe(iii) detection, J. Mater. Chem. A. 3 (2015) 542–546. doi: 10.1039/C4TA05483K.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A colorimetric and fluorometric dual-mode carbon dots probe derived from phenanthroline precursor for the selective detection of Fe2+ and Fe3+
Anal. Sci.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T. Jiang, J. Huang, G. Ran, Q. Song, C. Wang, A colorimetric and fluorometric dual-mode carbon dots probe derived from phenanthroline precursor for the selective detection of Fe2+ and Fe3+, Anal. Sci. 39 (2023) 325–333. doi: 10.1007/s44211-022-00236-x.
